text: Whereas existing research explains the occurrence of intraparty competition through the channel of electoral rules and party statute provisions, this article digs deeper into the matter by focusing on the strategic expression of party policies. Our central contentions are as follows: (1) that electoral defeat increases heterogeneity in party preferences and (2) that politicians with policy preferences that are far away from the party’s median vote fewer ballots of fellow party members; (3) that the party membership gives politicians more discretion to reconcile conflicting party policies if the party is in power. We examine these assertions through an original data set we constructed over speeches of the party congresses of the CDU and the CSU on the one hand, and of the French parties on the other. Using a set of algorithms for the computer-assisted classification of text into categories, we assess the relative policy positions of each party representative who made a speech. Based on these measurements, we derive a detailed picture of the relationships between the party agendas as expressed at a Congress, the distribution of party power, and the underlying context variables, such as whether the party or parties concerned are in government and the state of the economy.
